<!-- Topic 4: Defining Member Functions -->
<!-- Total slides: 7 -->

# Defining Member Functions

---

## What is a method? {.smaller}

+ A **method** is a function that belongs to a class — it is always called on an object and has direct access to all of the class's private members.
+ Methods define what an object can *do*; data members define what an object *knows*.
+ Syntax: `object.methodName(arguments)` — the dot ties the call to a specific object instance.

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Inline or outside the class? {.smaller}

+ **Inline**: the function body is written *inside* the class definition — compact, good for short functions.
+ **Outside**: the class lists a *prototype* only; the body is defined below using `ClassName::methodName()`.

::: notes
Total slides: 5
Both forms compile and run identically — this is a style and organization choice. Small getters and setters are typically inline. Longer methods are defined outside to keep the class definition readable as an interface summary.
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Inline definition {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
class StopLight {
private:
    bool isPowered = false;
public:
    bool getState() const { return isPowered; }  // inline — body here
    void powerOn();                              // prototype only
    void powerOff();                             // prototype only
};
```

::: notes
getState() is short enough to live inline. powerOn() and powerOff() are declared with prototypes — their bodies will be written outside. The class definition now reads like a clean public interface.
:::

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Outside definition {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
class StopLight {
private:
    bool isPowered = false;
public:
    bool getState() const { return isPowered; }  // inline — body here
    void powerOn();                              // prototype only
    void powerOff();                             // prototype only
};

void StopLight::powerOn() {
    isPowered = true;
}

void StopLight::powerOff() {
    isPowered = false;
}

int main() {
    StopLight bidwell;
    bidwell.powerOn();
    cout << bidwell.getState() << "\n";   // 1
    bidwell.powerOff();
    cout << bidwell.getState() << "\n";   // 0
    return 0;
}
```

::: notes
The :: is the scope resolution operator. It tells the compiler which class this function belongs to. Without it, powerOn() would be a free function, not a member — and it wouldn't have access to isPowered.
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->

---

## Accessors (getters) {.smaller}

An accessor reads a private member without changing the object. Mark every accessor `const`.

```{.cpp}
class Temperature {
private: double degrees = 0.0;
public:  double getDegrees() const  { return degrees; }
};

class Person {
private: string name;
public:  string getName()   const   { return name; }
};

class Counter {
private: int count = 0;
public:  int  getCount()    const   { return count; }
         bool isEmpty()     const   { return count == 0; }  // derived fact
};

class BankAccount {
private: double balance = 0.0;
public:  double getBalance() const  { return balance; }
         bool   isOverdrawn() const { return balance < 0.0; }
};
```

<!-- Slide 5 -->

---

## Mutators (setters) {.smaller}

A mutator modifies a private member. Setters can validate before storing — this is the value of keeping data private.

```{.cpp}
class Temperature {
private: double degrees = 0.0;
public:  void setDegrees(double d)  { degrees = d; }              // simple assign
};

class Person {
private: string name;
public:  void setName(string n)     { name = n.empty() ? "Unknown" : n; }
};

class Counter {
private: int count = 0;
public:  void increment()           { count++; }                  // no parameter needed
         void reset()               { count = 0; }
         void setCount(int c)       { if (c >= 0) count = c; }   // reject negatives
};

class BankAccount {
private: double balance = 0.0;
public:  void deposit(double amt)   { if (amt > 0) balance += amt; }
         void withdraw(double amt)  { if (amt > 0 && amt <= balance) balance -= amt; }
};
```

::: notes
Key distinction: the setter decides whether to accept a value — the caller cannot write directly to balance or count, so garbage data can't slip in. This is the payoff for keeping members private.
:::

<!-- Slide 6 -->

---

## Summary {.smaller}

+ Member functions can be defined inline (inside the class body) or outside using `ClassName::method()`.
+ Accessors (getters) read private data — always `const`. Mutators (setters) modify it and can validate before storing.

<!-- Slide 7 -->
